Facts.text: 6th recorded perihelion passage of Halley's Comet
Parliament of Paris begins persecuting Protestants
Duke of Alva leads "tenth penning" in Les Ponts de Cé
The Linköping Bloodbath takes place on Maundy Thursday in Linköping, Sweden
United Dutch East Indian Company (VOC) is formed and granted a monopoly on all Dutch sea-borne trade with Asia
France and Spain sign an accord to fight Protestantism
Dutch sea captain Willem de Vlamingh returns to Batavia after exploring "South Land" (west coast of Australia)
Akō incident: 46 of the 47 surviving Ronin commit seppuku (ritual suicide) as recompense for avenging their master's death in Edo
Iranian ruler Nadir Shah occupies Delhi in India and sacks the city, stealing the jewels of the Peacock Throne
Great Fire of Boston destroys 349 buildings
French army defeats Turks at Helipolis & advance to Cairo
Lady Hester Stanhope sets out for ancient city of Palmyra, the first western woman to visit
Prince Willem Frederik becomes monarch of Netherlands
US Supreme Court affirms its right to review state court decisions
US and Siam sign the Treaty of Amity and Commerce establishing friendly relations and commerce between the two states
Boston Public Library opens in Boston, Massachusetts as the first large free municipal library in the US
Republican Party is formally organized in Ripon, Wisconsin
An earthquake completely destroys Mendoza, Argentina
2nd day of Battle of Bentonville, North Carolina
Michigan authorizes workers' cooperatives
Jan Matzeliger receives his 1st patent (number 274,207) for shoe lasting machine which mechanized shoe production
Unity treaty of Paris signed: protects industrial property
1st AC power plant in US begins commercial operation in Massachusetts
Premiere of the very first Romani language operetta staged in Moscow, Russia
General Federation of Women's Clubs founded in the United States
Marines land in Nicaragua to protect US citizens
Uprising in Matabeleland
1st US orthodox Jewish Rabbinical seminary (RIETS) incorporates in NY
France and Russia issue a joint declaration that approves the Anglo-Japanese Alliance, but stipulates that they have the right to protect interests in China and Korea
National Squash Tennis Association forms (NYC)
Winter Garden Theater opens at 1634 Broadway NYC
1st international figure-skating tournament held in US, New Haven
Allies attack Zeebrugge Belgium
1st flight from London to South Africa lands (took 1½ months)
Upper Silesia votes for amalgamation with Germany in a plebiscite that is 63% in favor
USS Langley is commissioned, US Navy's 1st aircraft carrier
WIP-AM in Philadelphia PA begins radio transmissions
Bavarian minister of interior refuses to forbid Nazi Sturm Abteilung
Belgian Senate rejects Dutch University in Ghent
Finnair begins scheduled flight of Helsinki-Tallinn
American engine builder Clessie Cummins sets diesel engine speed record of 80.4 mph (129.39 kph)
Bishop Schreiber warns against national-socialism in Berlin
Kara-Kalpak Autonomous Region in RSFSR becomes Kara-Kalpak ASSR
Legendary Australian race horse Phar Lap's last race, winning the Agua Caliente Handicap in Mexico in record time
Rudolf Kuhnold demonstrates radar in Kiel Germany
7,000 Jews flee German occupied Memel Lithuania
Paul Reynoud becomes French premier
Exhibition "Planning and Construction in the East" opens in Berlin to showcase Generalplan Ost (plan for the East) German rural settlements after planned deportation of millions of Slavs
Nazi German-Yugoslav pact drawn
Convoy PQ13 departs Reykjavik Iceland to Russia
Major German assault on Malta
German U-384 bombed & sinks
2,500 women trample guards and floorwalkers to purchase 1,500 alarm clocks announced for sale in a Chicago Illinois department store
Bus falls off bridge into Passaic River NJ, killing 16
US 70th Infantry Division captures Saarbrucken, immediately prior the invasion of Germany by the western Allies
180 tonne blue whale (record) caught in South Atlantic
Fujiyoshida, a city located in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan, in the center of the Japanese main island of Honshū is founded
Indonesian army offensive against Darul Islam on Java
Final ratification of peace treaty restoring sovereignty to Japan by US Senate
1st newspaper vending machine used (Columbia Pennsylvania)
KXTV TV channel 10 in Sacramento, CA (CBS) begins broadcasting
Edward Ochab succeeds Bolesław Bierut as 1st secretary of the Polish Communist Party
Mount Bezymianny on Kamchatka Peninsula (USSR) erupts
Tunisia gains independence from France when the Protocol agreement signed between the two countries
Union workers ended a 156-day strike at Westinghouse Electric Corp
USSR performs nuclear test
Britain accepts NATO offer to mediate in Cyprus, but Greece rejects it
50 inches of snow falls across the Mason–Dixon line
Clandestine Burasi Bizim Radio (communist) begins transmitting
Greek Clandestine Radio (communist), Voice of Truth 1st transmission
Sjoukje Dijkstra becomes world champion figure skater
1st "Pop Art" exhibition (NYC)
European Space Research Organization (ESRO) is established
Venkataraghavan takes 8-72 v NZ at Delhi
WOET (now WPTD) TV channel 16 in Dayton, OH (PBS) begins broadcasting
Military intervene in South-Yemen (leftist ministers resign)
Boston Bruins win 13th straight NHL game
19 mountain climbers killed on Japan's Mount Fuji during an avalanche

Donegall Street bombing: the Provisional Irish Republican Army detonate its first car bomb on Donegall Street in Belfast; four civilians, two RUC officers and a UDR soldier killed while 148 people were wounded
S Mansholt succeeds Malfatti as chairman of European Committee
NBC TV premiere of pilot episode of "Police Story", based on Los Angeles Police Dept. Joseph Wambaugh's writings
"The Super Cops" directed by Gordon Parks premieres in NYC, New York
Jevgeni Kulikov skates world record 1000m (1:15.70)
Communists and socialists win French municipal elections
Flyers' Rick MacLeash scores on 6th penalty shot against Islanders
The Mi Amigo ship containing England's pirate radio Caroline sinks
US appeals to International Court on hostages in Iran
Argentine ex-president Isabel Peron sentenced to 8 years
Jean Harris sentenced 15-to-life for slaying of Scarsdale Diet Dr
France performs nuclear test
Joan Jett & Blackhearts' "I Love Rock 'n' Roll" goes #1 for 7 weeks
Rev Andries Treurnicht forms Conservative Party of South Africa
Andy Kaufman and Fred Blassie's film "My Breakfast With Blassie" premieres
Nigerian Major-General Babatunde Idiagbon launches a campaign on ‘National Consciousness and Enlightenment’
US Senate rejects amendment to permit spoken prayer in public schools
Libby Riddles is 1st woman to win Iditarod Trail Dog Sled Race
228 km/h gust of wind strikes Cairngorm (UK record)
Jacques Chiracforms French government
New York City passes its first lesbian and gay rights legislation
FDA approves sale of AZT (AIDS treatment)
NASA launches Palapa B2P
Soap opera "Capitol" final episode
Soviet filmmakers arrive in Hollywood for an entertainment summit
Battle of Afabet: Eritrean People's Liberation Front enters the town of Afabet after defeating the Nadew Command during the Eritrean War of Independence
David Henry Hwang's "M. Butterfly," premieres in NYC
Richard J Kerr replaces Robert M Gates as deputy director of CIA
Supreme Court rules unanimously employers can't exclude women from jobs where exposure to toxic chemicals could potentially damage fetus
US forgives $2 billion in loans to Poland
Janice Pennington is awarded $1.3M for accident on Price is Right set
IRA-bomb kills 3-year-old in Warrington, England
El Salvador's 1st presidential election following 12-year-old civil war
Mashonaland U-24 beat Matabeleland on 1st inning to win Logan Cup
Zulu-king Goodwill Zwelithini founds realm in South Africa
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits record 4083.68
Members of the Japanese cult Aum Shinrikyo release sarin gas on three lines of the Tokyo subway, killing 13 people and injuring over 1,000
"Love Thy Neighbor" opens at Booth Theater NYC
Erik and Lyle Menendez are found guilty of killing their parents
UK admits humans can catch CJD (Mad Cow Disease)
US tobacco company Liggett admits cigarettes are addictive
Legoland California, the first Legoland outside Europe, opens in Carlsbad, California
Jamil Abdullah Al-Amin, a former Black Panther once known as H. Rap Brown, is captured after a gun battle that leaves a Georgia sheriff's deputy dead
Petrobras 36 Oil Platform, the world's largest oil rig, sinks with 400,000 US gallons of fuel and crude oil aboard, after suffering three explosions on March 15
Magnitude 6.6 earthquake hits Fukuoka, Japan, in its first major quake in over 100 years. One person is killed, and hundreds are injured and evacuated
Cyclone Larry makes landfall in eastern Australia, destroying most of the country's banana crop
50 people are killed and 240 injured in a wave of terror attacks across 10 cities in Iraq
Disney movie John Carter records one of the largest losses in cinema history with a $200 million dollar write down
First Breakthrough Prizes, world's most generous science prize worth $3 million, awarded in Mathematics, Life Sciences and Physics established by Julia and Yuri Milner
Pierre Deligne wins the 2013 Abel Prize in mathematics
Indian rivers Yamuna and Ganges are declared "living entities" by a court in the state of Uttarakhand
A woman who can smell Parkinson's disease has helped researchers identify molecules on the skin of people with the disease in Manchester, England
Finland is the world's happiest country, South Sudan is world's least happy, according to annual World Happiness Report
Four men arrested in South Korea for secretly filming 1,600 hotel guests and selling the footage via a website
Nebraska Governor Pete Ricketts calls the state's flooding "the most widespread destruction we have ever seen in our state's history"
India hangs four men for 2012 gang rape and murder of woman on a bus in New Delhi, country's first hanging since 2013
Smoke from Australian bushfires killed more people than the fires - 417 vs 33 according to new study published in "Medical Journal of Australia"
Miami Beach imposes a state of emergency and a curfew as large crowds descend on the area for spring break
Overseas spectators will not be allowed to attend the Tokyo Summer Olympics due to the pandemic Japanese organizers confirm
Severe one-in-a-hundred-year flooding in Sydney and the surrounding state of New South Wales prompts evacuation orders
Intense fighting in Ukrainian city of Mariupol continues as Russian forces encircle the city, trapping 300,000 people
Somalian drought may have killed 43,000 people in 2022, half of them children under five, according to new research presented by UNICEF in Mogadishu
Sri Lanka receives bailout from the International Monetary Fund (IMF) worth $2.9bn, amid its worst financial crisis since independence in 1948

UN's Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change says world has less than a decade to stop catastrophic warming: must reduce greenhouse gases by half by 2030, and cease adding carbon dioxide to the atmosphere by early 2050s
French mathematician Michel Talagrand awarded 2024 Abel prize for his work on probability theory and describing randomness
Irish Prime Minister Leo Varadkar, the country's first gay and biracial taoiseach, announces he is stepping down after serving 2017-2020 and since December 2022
A wildfire ignites in Uiseong county, South Korea, burns for 10 days killing 30 people and destroying 4,000 structures including UNESCO World Heritage sites
Former swimmer Kirsty Coventry of Zimbabwe is the first woman and the first African to be elected president of the International Olympic Committee
Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vorders all Lutheran books in the Low Countries to be "reduced to ash"
Governor of Brittany, Philippe Emmanuel the Duke of Mercœur submits to French KingHenry IVat Angers
Walter Raleighis released from the Tower of London to seek gold in Guiana
KingCharles Iof England first tries to escape captivity at Carisbrooke Castle on the Isle of Wight by climbing out of a window but gets stuck
ScientistRobert Hookeis appointed Professor of Geometry at Gresham College, London
Alessandro Voltareports his discovery of the electric battery in a letter toJoseph Banks, president of the Royal Society of London
Napoleon Bonaparteenters Paris after his escape from Elba and begins his 100-day rule

Anti-slavery activists within the US Whig political party opposed to the Kansas-Nebraska Act form a new Republican Party; notable politicians who switched allegiance includeAbraham Lincoln,Rutherford B. Hayes,Chester A. Arthur, andBenjamin Harrison
Jesse JamesGang robs a bank in Russellville, Kentucky, of $14,000
France signs treaty with EmperorMenelik IIof Abyssinia establishing a common border between French held Djibouti and Ethiopia
US Secretary of StateJohn Hayannounces that all nations to whom he sent notes calling for an 'open door' policy in China have essentially accepted his stance
George Bernard Shaw's play "Captain Brassbound's Conversion" premieres in London
After the sinking of 3 more American merchant ships, US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonmeets with cabinet, who agree that war is inevitable
American fast food restaurant chain Kentucky Fried Chicken (KFC) is founded as Sanders Court & Café byColonel Harland Sandersin North Corbin, Kentucky
American all-round female super athleteBabe Didrikson Zahariaspitches a hitless inning for Philadelphia A's in their exhibition pre-season baseball game against Brooklyn Dodgers
Franco's Republican army launch their (eventually successful) counter-offensive against Italian International Brigades fighting for the Nationalists at the Battle of Guadalajara, Spain
20th Academy Awards: "Gentleman's Agreement",Loretta Young,Ronald Colmanwin
First live televised symphony performances:Eugene Ormandyleads Philadelphia Orchestra on CBS, followed 90 minutes later byArturo Toscaninileading the NBC Orchestra on NBC
24th Academy Awards: "An American in Paris",Humphrey BogartandVivien Leighwin
Civil and Women's Rights ActivistDorothy Heighthas her first column published in the weekly African-American newspaper called the "New York Amsterdam News"
The Supremesrelease single "The Happening"
LBJsigns a bill removing gold backing from US paper money
Northern Ireland Prime MinisterJames Chichester-Clarkresigns in protest at what he views as a limited security response by the British government
Pittsburgh Pirates right fielderRoberto Clementeis elected to Baseball's Hall of Fame 11 weeks after his death in a plane crash
American publishing heiressPatty Hearstconvicted of armed robbery for her part in a 1974 California heist
Indian Prime MinisterIndira Gandhi's party loses election in India
Parisians elect former PMJacques Chiracas 1st mayor in a century
In a clash of tennis legends,Martina NavratilovaoutclassesChris EvertLloyd 6-2, 6-0 to win her first of 5 straight WTA Tour Championships at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Yvonne van Gennipskates ladies' world record 5 km (7:20.36)
Defending championMike Tysonbeats Tony Tubbs by TKO in round 2 at the Tokyo Dome, Tokyo for the undisputed world heavyweight boxing title
Major League Baseball announces Reds managerPete Roseis under investigation
LA Lakers retiresKareem Abdul-Jabbar's #33
SingerGloria Estefanfractures her spine when a truck hits her tour bus near Scranton, Pennsylvania
Court awards singer-songwriterPeggy Lee$3 million in suit against Disney regarding home video royalties
Michael Jacksonsigns a $65 million deal with Sony Records to produce six albums
Dan Jansenskates world record 500m (36.02 sec)
Beatles release single, a cover of Shirelles song, "Baby It's You" (written byBurt Bacharach& Mack David), with lateJohn Lennonas lead singer; 1st Fab Four single in nearly 10 years
PopeJohn Paul IIvisits Holy Land - Jordan, Israel, Palestine
Stephen Harperwins the leadership of the newly created Conservative Party of Canada, becoming the party's first leader
Over 150 Chadian soldiers are killed in eastern Chad by members of the rebel UFDC in an attempt to overthrow PresidentIdriss Déby
ActressCynthia Nixonannounces she will run for New York Governor
Saudi Arabia’s crown princeMohammed bin Salmanmeets with US PresidentDonald Trumpat the White House
The Walt Disney Company acquiresRupert Murdoch's 21st Century Fox entertainment business for $71 billion
After 20 years with the New England Patriots, six-time Super Bowl winning quarterbackTom Bradyofficially agrees to move to the Tampa Bay Buccaneers on a 2-year $50m guaranteed deal
Chinese leaderXi Jinpingvisits Russian leaderVladimir Putinin Moscow, the first between the two since Russia invaded Ukraine a year ago
British singer-songwriterElton Johnand lyricist Bernie Taupin receive Library of Congress Gershwin Prize at the White House in Washington, D.C.
New rule limiting tailpipe pollution aimed at greatly expanding electric vehicles in the US is announced by PresidentJoe Biden
Vaughan Gethingis confirmed as Wales' new First Minister and the first Black leader of a national government in Europe

Conjunction of Mars, Jupiter and Saturn, thought by scholars at the University of Paris to be the "cause of the plague epidemic" known as the Black Death. Actual cause was the bacterium yersinia pestis spread by fleas, rats and other animals.
The British parliament passes first of the Intolerable Acts: the Boston Port Act, which closed Boston harbor until colonists would pay for damages following the Boston Tea Party
London’s famous Burlington Arcade opens, the world’s 1st shopping arcade
Harriet Beecher Stowe's anti-slavery novel "Uncle Tom's Cabin" is published in Boston
GeneralDouglas MacArthurvows "I came through and I shall return" after escaping Japanese-occupied Philippines
Manuel Noriega's wife Felicidad arrested for stealing buttons from dresses
A US-led coalition launches a ground invasion of Iraq after an ultimatum forSaddam Husseinand his sons to leave Iraq expires
Barack Obamabecomes the first US President to visit Cuba since 1928, arriving for a three-day tour with First Lady Michelle
Ovid [Publius Ovidius Naso], Roman poet famous for writing "Metamorphoses", born in Sulmo, Italy, Roman Republic (d. 17 AD)
Princess Cecily of York, third daughter of King Edward IV of England and his queen consort Elizabeth Woodville, born in Westminster Palace, London (d. 1507)
Jerome Emser, German theologian and antagonist of Martin Luther, born in Ulm, Germany (d. 1527)
Pierino Belli, Italian soldier and jurist, born in Alba, Italy (d. 1575)
Balthasar Bekker, Dutch theologist (helped end persecution of witches), born in Metslawier (d. 1698)
Abdul Hamid I, 27th Sultan of the Ottoman Empire (1774-89), born in Topkapi Palace, Constantinople, Ottoman Empire (d. 1789)
Torbern Bergman, Swedish chemist and mineralogist notable for publishing the largest chemical affinity tables, born in Katrineberg, Sweden (d. 1784)
Rama I [Phra Phutthayotfa Chulalok], King of Thailand (1782-1809) and founder of the Chakri dynasty, born in Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya, Thailand (d. 1809)
Eligio Celestino, Italian violinist and composer, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1812)
Jean-Antoine Houdon, French neoclassical sculptor known for his sculptures of famous public figures (George Washington, Benjamin Franklin), born in Versailles, France (d. 1828)[1][2]
Martinus van Marum, Dutch physician and inventor (Homo Diluvii Testus, constructed largest electrostatic machine in the world), born in Delft, Netherlands (d. 1837)
Friedrich Hölderlin, German poet, dramatist, and philosopher, born in Lauffen am Neckar, Duchy of Württemberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1843)
John Braham, English tenor singer and composer, born in London (d. 1856)
Neal Dow, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Portland, Maine (d. 1897)
George Caleb Bingham, American politician and painter (Country Election), born in Augusta, County, Virginia (d. 1879)
Napoleon II, son of Napoleon Bonaparte, Prince Imperial and King of Rome (never ruled France), born in Tuileries Palace, Paris, France (d. 1832)
George Bibb Crittenden, American soldier, Major General in Confederate Army, born in  Russellville, Kentucky (d. 1880)
Matthias Keller, German hymn writer (O Farther un High), born in Ulm, Württemberg (d. 1875)
Alexandru Ioan Cuza, 1st Prince of united Romania (land reform, emancipated peasants), born in Huşi, Moldavia (d. 1873)
John Echols, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Lynchburg, Virginia (d. 1896)
Ned Buntline [Edward Judson Sr.], American publisher, journalist, and writer, born in Harpersfield, New York (d. 1886)
William Beall, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army, relief efforts for prisoners), born in Bardstown, Kentucky (d. 1883)
Carel Vosmaer, Dutch poet and art critic (Netherlands Spectator), born in The Hague (d. 1888)
Charles Albert White, American popular song composer ("Marguerite"), born in Taunton, Massachusetts (d. 1892)
Eugene Asa Carr, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Hamburg, New York (d. 1910)
Solomon L. Spink, American lawyer and member of the Illinois House of Representatives, born in Whitehall, New York (d. 1881)
Henry Southwick Perkins, American composer, educator, and founder of Music Teachers' National Association, born in Stockbridge, Vermont (d. 1914)
Charles William Eliot, American academic (President of Harvard University, 1869-1909), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1926)
Edward Poynter, British painter, President of the Royal Academy, born in Paris, France (d. 1919)
Ferris Jacobs, Jr., American military officer and politician (US Representative from New York 1881-83), born in Delhi, New York (d. 1886)
Franz Mertens, Polish mathematician, born in Schroda, Grand Duchy of Posen, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1927)
Illarion Pryanishnikov, Russian painter and co-founder of the Peredvizhniki artistic cooperative, born in Borovsky Uyezd, Russian Empire (d. 1894)
Gavriil Musicescu, Romainian composer, born in Izmail, Bessarabia Governorate, Russian Empire (now Ukraine) (d. 1903)
George Godfrey, Canadian boxer ("Old Chocolate" - World Colored Heavyweight Champion 1883-88), born in Charlottetown, Prince Edward Island (d. 1901)
Sir John Lavery, Irish wartime painter, born in Belfast, Ireland (d. 1941)
Ernesto Nazareth, Brazilian pianist dance and classical music composer, born in Rio de Janeiro, Empire of Brazil (d. 1934)
Robert Poore, Irish cricket batsman (3 Tests for South Africa; Hampshire), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1938)
Paul von Lettow-Vorbeck 'the Lion of Africa', German general and politician (only German to successfully invade part of British empire in WWI), born in Saarlouis, Prussia (d. 1964)
Joe McGinnity, American Baseball Hall of Fame pitcher (World Series 1905; 5 × NL wins leader; NL ERA leader 1904; Baltimore Orioles, NY Giants), born in Rock Island, Illinois (d. 1929)
Bernhard Seklas, German pianist, composer, conductor, and pedagogue thought to be the first jazz instructor, born in Frankfurt am Main, Germany (d. 1934)
Karin Michaelis, Danish writer (The Dangerous Age), born in Randers, Denmark (d. 1950)
Börries von Münchhausen, German poet and Nazi activist, born in Hildesheim, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1945)
William Payne Whitney, American businessman and Whitney family member, born in New York (d. 1927)
Maud Menten, Canadian biochemist, known for the Michaelis–Menten equation, born in Port Lambton, Ontario (d. 1960)
Eugène Schueller, French chemist, founder of L'Oréal, born in Paris, France (d. 1957)
René Coty, French politician and President of France (1954-59), born in Le Harve, France (d. 1960)[1]
Colin Campbell, Scottish actor (High Bright Sun, Leather Boys), born in Falkirk, Scotland (d. 1966)
Karl Hasse, German composer, born in Dohna, Kingdom of Saxony, German Empre (d. 1960)
Eugen Herrigel, German philosopher (early pioneer of Zen in Europe), born in Lichtenau, Germany (d. 1955)
Siegfried von Vegesack, German writer, born in Gut Blumbergshof, Latvia (d. 1974)
Lauritz Melchior, Danish-American baritone tenor (National Symphony), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1973)
Edmund Goulding, British screenwriter and film director (Grand Hotel, Dark Victory, Nightmare Alley), born in Feltham, England (d. 1959)
Fredric Wertham, German-born American psychiatrist and author, born in Nuremberg, German Empire (d. 1981)
Hal Walker, director (I Married Joan), born in Ottumwa, Iowa (d. 1972)
Sam Wren, American actor (Marked Woman, The Old Grey Mayor), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1962)
David Lasser, American science fiction magazine editor (Wonder Stores, 1930-33), writer (The Conquest of Space), and workers' rights activist, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1996)
Kathryn Forbes, American short story writer (mama's bank account), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1966)
Edgar Buchanan, American actor (Petticoat Junction), born in Humansville, Missouri (d. 1979)
Vincent Richards, American tennis player (US Pro C'ships 1927-28, 30, 33), born in Yonkers, New York (d. 1959)
B F Skinner, American psychologist (Skinner Box), born in Susquehanna, Pennsylvania (d. 1990)
Jean Galia, French rugby player and boxer, established rugby league in France, International Hall of Fame 1988, born in Ille-sur-Têt, France (d. 1949)[1]
Vera Panova, Russian author and journalist (Sputniki), born in Rostov-on-Don, Russia (d. 1973)
Abraham Beame, American politician (Mayor of New York City, 1974-77, 1st Jewish mayor of the city), born in London, England (d. 2001)
Ozzie Nelson, American big band leader, radio and television actor and producer (The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 1975)
Pavel Parenago, Soviet scientist and astronomer (stellar astronomy), born in Krasnodar, Russia (d. 1960)
Frank Stanton, American broadcasting executive (CBS), born in Muskegon, Michigan (d. 2006)
Michael Redgrave, British actor (Browning Version, Goodbye Mr Chips, Lady Vanishes), born in Bristol, England (d. 1985)
Alfonso García Robles, Mexican diplomat and Nobel Peace Prize laureate 1982, lauded for his role in making Latin America nuclear-free, born in Zamora, Mexico (d. 1991)[1]
Mieke Verstraete [Maria Augustina De Graef], Belgian-Dutch actress (Pleasantly Settled), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1990)
Judith Evelyn [Evelyn Morris], American actress (13th Letter, Tingler, Rear Window), born in Seneca, South Dakota (d. 1967)
Wendell Corey, American actor (Rear Window, 11th Hour, Peck's Bad Girl), born in Dracut, Massachusetts (d. 1968)
William Baddeley, English church minister (restored St James's Piccadilly, Bishop of Brisbane), born in London, England (d. 1998)
Rudolf Kirchschläger, Austrian, judge, diplomat, and President of Austria (1974-86), born in Niederkappel, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 2000)[1]
Sister Rosetta Tharpe [Nubin], American gospel and R&B singer, songwriter, and electric guitarist ("This Train"; "Down By The Riverside"), born in Cotton Plant, Arkansas, (d. 1973)
Stan Spence, American baseball center fielder (MLB All-Star 1942, 44, 46, 47; Boston Red Sox, Washington Senators, St. Louis Browns), born in South Portsmouth, Kentucky (d. 1983)
Sviatoslav Richter, Soviet pianist considered one of the greatest pianists of the 20th century, born in Zhytomyr, Ukraine, Russian Empire (d. 1997)
Pierre Messmer, French politician (Prime Minister of France 1972-74 under Georges Pompidou), born in  Vincennes, France (d. 2007)
Peter Caddy, British caterer and hotelier (co-founder of the Findhorn Community), born in London (d. 1994)
Vera Lynn, British popular music singer, known as 'the Forces' Sweetheart' ("We'll Meet Again"; "The White Cliffs of Dover"), born in East Ham, London (d. 2020)
Bernd-Alois Zimmermann, German composer (The Soldiers; Requiem), born in Bliesheim, Germany (d. 1970)
Donald Featherstone, British writer and wargamer, born in London, England (d. 2013)
Jack Barry, American game show emcee (Joker's Wild), born in Lindenhurst, New York (d. 1984)
Marian McPartland, British jazz pianist and radio broadcaster (Marian McPartland's Piano Jazz), born in Slough, England (d. 2013)
Douglas G. Chapman, Canadian-American biomathematical statistician (whale conservation), born in Provost, Alberta (d. 1996)[1]
Amadou-Mahtar M'Bow, Senegalese UNESCO Director General UNESCO (1974-87), born in Dakar, French West Africa

Primož Ramovš, Slovenian avant-garde composer (Za Harmoniko; Tryptychon), pedagogue, and library director (Slovenian Academy of Sciences and Arts, 1945-87), born in Ljubljana, Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (d. 1999)
Arnold Burgen, British physician and founding President  of Academia Europaea, born in London, England (d. 2022)
Doug Barnard Jr, American politician (Rep-D-GA, 1977-93), born in Augusta, Georgia (d. 2018)
Jack Kruschen, Canadian character actor (The Apartment, Busting Loose), born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 2002)
Larry Elgart, American big band jazz alto saxophonist, and bandleader ("Bandstand Boogie"; "Hooked On Swing"), born in New London, Connecticut (d. 2017)
Ray Goulding, American comedian (Bob & Ray), born in Lowell, Massachusetts (d. 1990)
Shaukat Siddiqui, Pakistani author, journalist, and political activist (God's Own Land, Jangloos), born in Lucknow, British India (d. 2006)[1][2]
Hermann Guggiari, Paraguayan engineer and sculptor (Rejas; NNUU), born in Asunción, Paraguay (d. 2012)
Jozef Kroner, Slovak actor (The Shop on Main Street), born in Staškov, Slovakia (d. 1998)
David Warren, Australian scientist and inventor (Flight Data Recorder; Cockpit Voice Recorder - "the black box"), born in Groote Eylandt, Australia (d. 2010)
John Erlichman, American politician aid to Nixon (convicted over Watergate break-in), born in Tacoma, Washington (d. 1999)
Harold Rosen, American engineer (created geosynchronous communications satellite), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2017)[1]
John Joubert, British South African composer (The Raising of Lazarus; An English Requiem), and educator, born in Cape Town, Union of South Africa (d. 2019)
Anthony Blond, British publisher (The Publishing Game, Book Book), born in Sale, Cheshire (d. 2008)
Jerome Biffle, American long jumper (Olympic gold, 1952), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 2002)
Germán Robles, Spanish-Mexican film, theater, television, and voice actor (El Vampiro), born in Gijón, Asturias, Spain (d. 2015)
Michel Magne, French experimental music and film score composer (Gigot; Barbarella), and recording studio owner (Château d'Hérouville), born in Lisieux, France (d. 1984)
Antonio Tauriello, Argentine composer, pianist, and conductor, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina
Hal Linden [Harold Lipshitz], American actor (Barney Miller; Blacke's Magic), director, and musician, born in The Bronx, New York
John Rae, British educator (Headmaster of Westminster, 1970-86), writer (Conscience & Politics), and novelist (The Custard Boys), born in London (d. 2006)
